title: Fast single-atom preparation in optical tweezers via Rydberg blockade
authors: Yiyi Li, Vernon M. Hughes, Michael Peper, Yicheng Bao, Chenyuan Li, Sanzhar Bissenali, Jeff D. Thompson
abstract: Continuously replenished optical tweezer arrays will unlock unlimited-depth quantum circuits with neutral atom qubits. A key bottleneck limiting the cycle time of these systems is removing atoms from tweezers initially loaded with more than one atom. In the conventional technique of light-assisted collisions, slow collisional dynamics limit the timescale for removing excess atoms to several milliseconds. Here, we propose and demonstrate a scheme for selectively removing one atom at a time from multiply occupied tweezers on a microsecond timescale, using intra-tweezer Rydberg blockade and autoionization. We demonstrate the protocol in $^{171}$Yb in two complementary regimes. With two-photon Rydberg excitation from the ground state, we reduce multi-atom probability to 1% in 64.8 $μ$s, while retaining single atoms in 58.2(2)% of the tweezers, which is comparable to the filling fraction achieved with light-assisted collisions under the same experimental conditions, but over two orders of magnitude faster. With single-photon excitation from the metastable state $^3P_0$, reduced single-atom loss enables a higher filling fraction of 74.8(3)%, at the cost of additional temporal overhead to prepare the atoms in $^3P_0$. The final filling fraction is limited by an unexplained two-body loss mechanism, which, if solved, could enable fast, quasi-deterministic loading.
summary: This paper presents a novel, fast protocol for preparing single atoms in optical tweezer arrays by leveraging intra-tweezer Rydberg blockade and autoionization. By operating on a microsecond timescale, the method significantly overcomes the slow cycle time limitations of current quantum circuit implementations. The research highlights the critical role of minimizing multi-atom loss to achieve scalable, high-fidelity quantum computation.
main_problem: The primary bottleneck in continuously replenished optical tweezer arrays for quantum circuits is the slow cycle time associated with removing excess atoms from multiply occupied tweezers.
main_result: The authors demonstrated a microsecond-scale protocol using intra-tweezer Rydberg blockade and autoionization, achieving single-atom filling fractions up to 74.8% and showing a significant speedup over conventional methods.
method: They propose and experimentally demonstrate a selective 'remove-one' protocol by cycling Rydberg excitation and autoionization to reduce multi-atom occupancy rapidly.
model_or_system: The system involves neutral atoms (specifically Yb) trapped in optical tweezer arrays. The physics relies on controlled Rydberg blockade interactions and autoionization dynamics.
key_observables: Single-atom filling fraction ($P^*(1)$), multi-atom probability ($P(>1)$), and the time scale required to reach a target occupancy.
important_parameters: The protocol operates on the microsecond timescale, contrasting with millisecond timescales of previous methods; key regimes include two-photon vs. single-photon excitation.
main_assumptions: The final filling fraction is limited by an unexplained two-body loss mechanism ($N 	o N-2$), which is the primary remaining challenge.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate collective Rabi oscillations for various atom numbers and show the time evolution of the filling fraction versus the target multi-atom probability threshold for both excitation regimes.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory and experimental demonstration by first establishing the need for fast cycling, then comparing two excitation regimes (two-photon vs. single-photon), and finally analyzing the limiting loss mechanisms and optimizing the pulse sequence.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the scalability bottleneck in neutral atom quantum computing platforms by providing a fast, high-fidelity method for preparing single-atom qubits in arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
